did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid deep learning model named MSLCKE, which combines GoogleNet and ResNet for feature extraction with an Improved Extreme Learning Machine (IELM) classifier, to perform multi-class skin lesion detection and classification on the HAM10000 dermatological dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a hybrid deep learning model named MSLCKE for multi-class skin lesion detection. It details the methodology (combining GoogleNet, ResNet, and IELM), describes the dataset used (HAM10000, ISIC 2016, etc.), and reports specific quantitative results (accuracy, precision, recall, computational time) compared against baseline models. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICESIC), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: An Early Detection of Multi-Class Skin Lesion Using Convolutional Neural Network Architecture and Kernel Soft Plus Extreme Learning
journal: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
brief_summary: This study proposes MSLCKE, a hybrid deep learning model for multi-class skin lesion detection that combines GoogleNet and ResNet for feature extraction with an Improved Extreme Learning Machine (IELM) classifier. The model was evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 dermatoscopic images of seven skin lesion classes, as well as the ISIC 2016, ACS, and PAD-UFES-20 datasets. The authors assessed performance using accuracy, precision, recall, computational time, and complexity metrics. MSLCKE achieved 98% accuracy on the HAM10000 dataset and 99% on the ISIC 2016 and ACS datasets, outperforming baseline models like IDAEM, PMLBM, and SDCTF. The method demonstrated superior computational efficiency, with processing times as low as 0.008 seconds and significantly reduced model complexity compared to competitors. The results indicate that MSLCKE offers a robust, low-complexity solution for early skin cancer diagnosis in resource-constrained clinical settings.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin lesion categories (Actinic keratosis, Basal cell carcinoma, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic nevus, Vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset (7 classes) and other datasets (ISIC 2016, ACS, PAD-UFES-20). The model MSLCKE uses GoogleNet and ResNet for feature extraction and an Improved Extreme Learning Machine (IELM) classifier for predicting discrete diagnostic categories. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, sensitivity, specificity, and F1-score across these classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: Human Against Machine with 10,000 training images database containing 10,015 dermatoscopic images of pigmented lesions on human skin, obtained from the Medical University of Vienna and a medical facility in Queensland, Australia. Diagnoses were verified via histopathological examination or expert consensus.

use.training: Used for training the MSLCKE model; images were pre-processed for size uniformity, noise removal, and lesion emphasis, resized from 512x512 to 256x256x3.
use.testing: Used for testing the MSLCKE model; evaluated for accuracy, recall, precision, computational time, and complexity.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2016
datasets_used.description: A public dermatological dataset used for evaluation of the MSLCKE model.

use.testing: Used for testing the MSLCKE model; achieved 99% accuracy, 98% recall, and 98% precision.

datasets_used.name: ACS
datasets_used.description: A dataset used for evaluation of the MSLCKE model.

use.testing: Used for testing the MSLCKE model; achieved 99% accuracy, 97% recall, and 97% precision.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A challenging dermatological dataset used for evaluation of the MSLCKE model.

use.testing: Used for testing the MSLCKE model; achieved 97% accuracy, 96% recall, and 95% precision.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate per-class and macro-averaged metrics including accuracy, precision, sensitivity (recall), specificity, and F1-score for multi-class classification performance assessment.
To calculate Macro Average Precision (MAP) and Macro Average Sensitivity (MAS) to give equal weight to all classes, addressing class imbalance issues.

statistical_methods_used.method: Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve and AUC-ROC
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To analyze classifier discrimination capabilities across varying classification thresholds, particularly in the context of class imbalances.
To evaluate performance on minority classes where accuracy might be deceptive due to class imbalance.
To represent false negative and false positive rates on a logarithmic scale to facilitate the selection of optimal operating points.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using absolute accuracy, precision, recall, computational time, and complexity metrics without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: MSLCKE achieved 98% accuracy, which was 2-8% higher than existing methods. MSLCKE achieved 98% recall. MSLCKE achieved 98% precision. Computational time was 0.019 seconds. Complexity was 0.22 at value 0 and 0.07 at value 10.

ISIC 2016.notes: MSLCKE achieved 99% accuracy, which was 4%, 5%, and 9% higher than IDAEM, PMLBM, and SDCTF respectively. MSLCKE achieved 98% recall. MSLCKE achieved 98% precision. Computational time was 0.008 seconds.

ACS.notes: MSLCKE achieved 99% accuracy, with an improvement of 3-7% compared to other models. MSLCKE achieved 97% recall. MSLCKE achieved 97% precision. Computational time was 0.012 seconds.

PAD-UFES-20.notes: MSLCKE achieved 97% accuracy on the challenging dataset. MSLCKE achieved 96% recall, which was 2-6% higher than competing methods. MSLCKE achieved 95% precision. Computational time was not explicitly stated for this dataset in the text, but MSLCKE is described as the fastest method across various datasets.

architecture_used.main_model: MSLCKE (Multi-Stage Learning Convolutional Network with Kernel-based Extreme Learning)

architecture_details.backbone: Hybrid CNN combining GoogleNet and ResNet for hierarchical feature extraction
architecture_details.google_net_details: 22 layers; uses 1x1 convolutions for dimensionality reduction, global average pooling, and auxiliary classifiers to reduce overfitting and vanishing gradients
architecture_details.res_net_details: Uses residual blocks with shortcut connections to address vanishing gradients and enable training of deep networks
architecture_details.ielm_details: Uses Softplus activation function; utilizes kernel mapping with a value of 1/v inserted for ELM; calculates output weights using Moore-Penrose generalized inverse of the hidden layer output matrix
activation_functions: SoftMax (in CNN output layer for probability distribution)
regularization: Dropout layers (mentioned as potentially included to prevent overfitting)

training_details.optimizer: Moore-Penrose generalized inverse (for IELM weight calculation)

classification_or_output_head.output_activation: SoftMax (for final class probabilities in the CNN component before IELM integration)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: MSLCKE (Multi-Stage Learning Convolutional Network with Kernel-based Extreme Learning)
preprocessing_techniques: Size uniformity adjustment (resizing images from 512x512 to 256x256x3)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper did not report specific data augmentation techniques (such as geometric transforms, color transforms, or GAN augmentation) used during the training of the MSLCKE model. The methodology section mentions preprocessing steps including size uniformity adjustment (resizing from 512x512 to 256x256x3), noise removal, and lesion emphasis, but does not describe explicit data augmentation strategies applied to the training data.

conclusions: The proposed MSLCKE hybrid model, which combines GoogleNet and ResNet for feature extraction with an Improved Extreme Learning Machine (IELM) classifier, achieves superior classification accuracy compared to baseline models (IDAEM, PMLBM, SDCTF) across multiple dermatological datasets.
MSLCKE achieved 98% accuracy on the HAM10000 dataset, 99% on the ISIC 2016 and ACS datasets, and 97% on the challenging PAD-UFES-20 dataset.

The model demonstrated high recall rates ranging from 96% to 98% and precision rates between 95% and 98% across the evaluated datasets, outperforming competing methods by margins of 2-9% in precision.

MSLCKE exhibits significantly reduced computational complexity (reducing complexity by 60-85% compared to competitors) and faster processing times (as low as 0.008 seconds) compared to standard deep learning models and other baselines.
The integration of deep CNN feature extraction with the IELM classifier effectively mitigates issues of overfitting and vanishing gradients while maintaining high accuracy.
The method is suitable for resource-constrained clinical settings due to its low computational requirements and scalability.
Future work will explore combining attention mechanisms, transformers, and self-supervised learning to enhance the detection of complex lesion patterns.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on retrospective, publicly available dermatological datasets (HAM10000, ISIC 2016, ACS, PAD-UFES-20) without any prospective clinical validation or real-world deployment testing.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance differences against baseline models.
The paper does not report specific data augmentation techniques used during training, raising potential concerns about overfitting given the class imbalance inherent in datasets like HAM10000.
Performance metrics are reported as aggregate values (e.g., macro-average accuracy) without providing per-class breakdowns, which may mask poor performance on minority classes.
The model was evaluated on datasets with known demographic biases (e.g., HAM10000 is predominantly derived from Caucasian populations), limiting generalizability to diverse skin types.
Computational efficiency claims (e.g., 0.008 seconds processing time) are not contextualized with hardware specifications, making reproducibility and comparison difficult.

The hybrid architecture combines GoogleNet and ResNet with an Improved Extreme Learning Machine (IELM); however, the specific mechanism for integrating these components and handling feature dimensionality mismatches is not fully detailed in the provided context.
